examTitle: Chapter 10: Pacemaker Rhythms and Bundle Branch Block

questions.question: Which pacemaker rhythm shows a pacing spike before the P wave, but NOT before the QRS complex?
options: Atrial pacemaker rhythm
Ventricular pacemaker
Atrioventricular pacemaker
Normal sinus rhythm
questions.explanation: An atrial pacemaker only paces the atria, so its spike appears right before the P wave it's triggering — the QRS complex that follows is conducted normally through the intrinsic system, with no spike of its own.

questions.question: Which pacemaker rhythm shows a pacing spike before the QRS complex, but NOT before the P wave?
options: Atrial pacemaker rhythm
Ventricular pacemaker
Atrioventricular pacemaker
Normal sinus rhythm
questions.explanation: A ventricular pacemaker only paces the ventricles, so its spike shows up right before the QRS complex — there's no atrial pacing spike since the atria are still depolarizing on their own (or not producing a visible P wave at all in some cases).

questions.question: Which pacemaker rhythm has a pacing spike before P waves AND before QRS complexes?
options: Atrial pacemaker rhythm
Ventricular pacemaker
Atrioventricular pacemaker
Wandering atrial pacemaker
questions.explanation: An atrioventricular (dual-chamber) pacemaker paces both the atria and ventricles in sequence, producing two distinct spikes — one just before the P wave, and another just before the QRS complex.

questions.question: Which pacemaker complication shows a pacing spike but no waveform immediately following it?
options: Failure to sense
Failure to capture
Oversensing
Normal sinus rhythm
questions.explanation: Failure to capture means the electrical spike fired, but it didn't successfully trigger the heart muscle to depolarize — so you see the spike on the tracing, but no corresponding P wave or QRS follows it.

questions.question: You observe a wide QRS complex while continuously monitoring a patient in lead II. Which lead placement is necessary to evaluate the location of blockage in the bundle branch system?
options: Lead I
Lead V4
Lead III
Lead V1
questions.explanation: V1 gives the clearest view for distinguishing right vs. left bundle branch block based on QRS morphology — lead II alone can flag that something's wide, but V1 is what actually helps pinpoint which side is blocked.

questions.question: The labeling of the ECG rhythm strip for documentation of the bundle branch block should include what other information besides which bundle is being blocked?
options: Symptoms the patient is experiencing
Blood pressure reading
Presence of an MI diagnosis
Patient's inherent rhythm pattern
questions.explanation: Documenting the patient's own underlying rhythm alongside the bundle branch block gives the physician the full clinical picture — the block is only part of the story; what the heart's baseline rhythm is doing matters too.

questions.question: When the QRS complex measures _____, bundle branch block must be considered.
options: 0.06 to 0.10 second
0.04 to 0.08 second
less than 0.06 second
0.12 second or greater
questions.explanation: A QRS of 0.12 seconds or wider signals delayed conduction through the ventricles — bundle branch block is one of the key possibilities to rule in or out whenever you see that widened complex.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the components to be evaluated on a pacemaker tracing?
options: The presence of atrial and/or ventricular spikes
The QT interval
The characteristic patterns of the chambers captured after the spikes
The atrioventricular delay period
questions.explanation: Evaluating a pacemaker tracing focuses on the pacing spikes themselves, whether capture actually occurred, and the AV delay timing — the QT interval isn't part of the specific pacemaker-function checklist.

questions.question: What is pacemaker competition?
options: A device that delivers electrical energy to cause depolarization
Rivalry between the intrinsic and electronic pacemakers, which can lead to life-threatening complications
Pacing activity occurs but is not captured by the myocardium
Pacemaker fails to detect any intrinsic electrical activity
questions.explanation: Pacemaker competition happens when the heart's own natural pacemaker and the electronic device are both trying to control the rhythm at the same time — a dangerous situation since competing impulses can land at vulnerable moments in the cardiac cycle.

questions.question: What is an electronic pacemaker?
options: Rivalry between the intrinsic and electronic pacemakers
Pacing activity occurs but is not captured by the myocardium
Device that delivers electrical energy to cause depolarization (contractions)
Pacemaker fails to send electrical impulse to the heart
questions.explanation: An electronic (artificial) pacemaker is the actual implanted device that delivers electrical impulses to stimulate the heart to contract when the intrinsic system isn't reliably doing the job.

questions.question: What is 'loss of capture'?
options: Pacemaker does not recognize the patient's inherent heart rate
Pacing activity occurs but is not captured by the myocardium
Electrical current from muscle movements or other activities are sensed by the pacemaker
Pacemaker fails to detect any intrinsic electrical activity
questions.explanation: Loss of capture is when the pacemaker fires a spike, but the heart muscle doesn't actually respond and depolarize — the same concept tested earlier as the 'spike with no waveform following it' complication.

questions.question: What is 'undersensing'?
options: Pacemaker fails to send electrical impulse to the heart
Electrical current causes the myocardial tissue to depolarize
Heart muscle responds to electrical stimulation and depolarizes
Pacemaker fails to detect any intrinsic electrical activity
questions.explanation: Undersensing means the pacemaker misses the heart's own natural electrical activity that's already happening — so it may fire unnecessarily, potentially competing with the heart's intrinsic rhythm.

questions.question: What is 'malsensing'?
options: Pacemaker does not recognize the patient's inherent heart rate
Device that delivers electrical energy to cause depolarization
Heart muscle responds to electrical stimulation and depolarizes
Electrical current causes the myocardial tissue to depolarize
questions.explanation: Malsensing is a broader sensing failure — the pacemaker isn't correctly recognizing the patient's own heart rate, which covers both undersensing and oversensing as specific subtypes.

questions.question: What does 'malfunctioning' mean, in the context of a pacemaker?
options: Pacemaker fails to send electrical impulse to the heart
Rivalry between the intrinsic and electronic pacemakers
Electrical current from muscle movements or other activities are sensed by the pacemaker
Heart muscle responds to electrical stimulation and depolarizes
questions.explanation: A malfunctioning pacemaker is one that fails to deliver its electrical impulse to the heart at all — a distinct problem from sensing issues, since here the device simply isn't firing when it should.

questions.question: What is 'oversensing'?
options: Pacemaker fails to detect any intrinsic electrical activity
Electrical current from muscle movements or other activities are sensed by the pacemaker
Device that delivers electrical energy to cause depolarization
Pacing activity occurs but is not captured by the myocardium
questions.explanation: Oversensing is the opposite problem from undersensing — the pacemaker picks up electrical 'noise' from things like muscle movement that aren't actually a real heartbeat, and may withhold pacing when it shouldn't.

questions.question: What does 'triggered' mean, in the context of pacemaker function?
options: Electrical current causes the myocardial tissue to depolarize (contract)
Rivalry between the intrinsic and electronic pacemakers
Pacemaker fails to send electrical impulse to the heart
Pacemaker does not recognize the patient's inherent heart rate
questions.explanation: Triggered describes the electrical event itself — the current causing the myocardial tissue to actually depolarize and contract, whether that trigger came from the pacemaker or the heart's own conduction system.

questions.question: What does 'capture' mean, in the context of pacemaker function?
options: Device that delivers electrical energy to cause depolarization
Pacing activity occurs but is not captured by the myocardium
Heart muscle responds to electrical stimulation and depolarizes (contracts)
Electrical current from muscle movements or other activities are sensed by the pacemaker
questions.explanation: Capture is the successful outcome — the heart muscle actually responding to the pacing spike and contracting, which is exactly what's missing in a 'loss of capture' or 'failure to capture' complication.
